summary:
The Windmill Hill culture (also called the Windmill Hill people or British Neolithic culture) refers to the early Neolithic farming communities of southern Britain, dated to approximately 4000–3300 BCE and named after the causewayed enclosure at Windmill Hill near Avebury in Wiltshire. These communities represent the first substantial farming populations of the British Isles, descendants of Neolithic settlers who crossed from continental Europe and established agricultural settlements across England, Wales, and Scotland.

Windmill Hill communities cultivated emmer and einkorn wheat, barley, and lentils, and herded cattle, pigs, sheep, and goats. Their characteristic monuments include causewayed enclosures — oval or circular enclosures defined by interrupted ditches and banks that served as seasonal gathering places for feasting, exchange, and ritual — and long barrows for collective burial of the dead. The long barrows, constructed of earth and sometimes chambered with stone, represent the British contribution to the broader [[event:35|megalithic tradition]] of Atlantic Europe.

Recent ancient DNA analysis has shown that Windmill Hill people were overwhelmingly descended from Anatolian Neolithic farmers who had spread through continental Europe before reaching Britain, with minimal genetic contribution from preceding Mesolithic hunter-gatherers. This population was subsequently largely replaced around 3000–2500 BCE by newcomers associated with the Beaker culture and steppe ancestry — one of the most dramatic demographic replacements documented in prehistoric Britain.
